t: — Intrusive memories, flashbacks, nightmares; acute distress and somatic reactions with triggers.
— avoidance of thoughts, feelings, reminders, situations related to trauma.
— Hypervigilance, easily startled, sleep disturbance, irritability.
Negative beliefs (about self, others, the world).
Numbing of feelings, persistent negative mood.
Symptoms ≥ 1 month; onset may be delayed after trauma (delayed onset > 6 months).
Exposure to traumatic event (personal, witnessing, information about death/injury of close ones, repeated exposure to traumatic information in occupational context).
ICD-11: persistent sense of threat; DSM-5-TR: cognitive-mood alterations + hyperarousal.
Exclusion of substance or medical condition.
4 symptom domains (intrusions, avoidance, cognitive-mood, hyperarousal); dissociative subtype qualifier (depersonalization/derealization); delayed onset.
3 symptom domains (more compact); complex PTSD as a separate category (6B42).
Gold standard diagnosis CAPS-5; PCL-5 screening in primary medical care.
CAPS-5 (Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale) — gold standard structured interview.
Comorbidity (MDD, GAD, substance, BPD, complex PTSD).
Except for medical and traumatic brain injury (TBI).
Acute stress disorder (similar to 6B40, DSM-5: ASD)
Does not meet trauma criterion A.
Affective symptoms; no intrusions and avoidance.
Persistent personality pattern; impulsivity and relationships.
Dissociation dominant; dissociative subtype may be a qualifier in PTSD.
8.1 General Principles (VA/DoD 2023 · APA 2017 · NICE NG116 Consensus)
— In vivo and imaginal exposure to traumatic memory.
EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing, Shapiro F.)
SSRI (sertraline and paroxetine — FDA approval for PTSD), SNRI (venlafaxine).
mirtazapine, nefazodone (risk of hepatotoxicity — use with caution).
— prazosin (α1 antagonist) — evidence base is controversial (Raskind 2003 positive, 2018 PACT trial neutral); individual response.
Treatment duration — psychotherapy 12–16 sessions; pharmacotherapy ≥ 12 months after remission.
VA/DoD 2023 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder and Acute Stress Disorder:

trauma-focused psychotherapy first-line (strong recommendation); pharmacotherapy first-line alternative; combination psychotherapy + pharmacotherapy not routine recommendation; benzodiazepines contraindicated.
CPT, PE, KDT, and EMDR are strongly recommended (same evidence base).
Trauma-focused CBT or EMDR; SSRIs second-line.
— Cochrane (Rose S. et al. 2002) and NICE do not explicitly recommend; some studies increase PTSD risk.

Imaginal exposure to traumatic memory (repeated, sequential description) + in vivo exposure (to avoided situations). 10–15 sessions. Evidence: Foa E.B. et al. RCTs; gold-standard effect in meta-analyses.
Cognitive restructuring — "stuck points" about trauma (safety, trust, power, self-esteem, intimacy). 12 sessions. Resick P.A. et al. RCTs.
Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) — Shapiro (Shapiro F.)
— Reprocessing traumatic memory with bilateral stimulation (eye movement, auditory, or tactile). 8-phase protocol. Evidence: Bisson J.I. et al. Cochrane 2013 — comparable effect to CBT.
Trauma-Focused Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (TF-CBT) — Cohen J., Mannarino A.
For children and adolescents aged 3–18 years; PRACTICE protocol (Psychoeducation, Relaxation, Affective modulation, Cognitive coping, Trauma narrative, In vivo, Conjoint sessions, Enhancing safety).
PTSD Checklist (PCL-5 — Posttraumatic Stress Disorder Checklist)
Trauma-focused psychotherapy yields significant improvement in most patients.
Comorbid MDD, substance use poor prognosis markers.
Myth 1: "PTSD only occurs in war veterans"

PTSD can develop following any traumatic event — sexual violence (~50% prevalence), physical assault, accidents, natural disasters, medical procedures. Prevalence among veterans is high, but it is also widespread in the general population.
Myth 2: "CISD (Critical Incident Stress Debriefing) prevents PTSD after trauma"
Cochrane (Rose S. et al. 2002) — CISD does not reduce PTSD risk, in some studies
. NICE and VA/DoD do not explicitly recommend. Early intervention as "watchful waiting" and trauma-focused CBT only for symptomatic patients.
Myth 3: "The patient should not talk about trauma, 'disclosure' is harmful"
Evidenced trauma-focused therapies (PE, CPT, EMDR) work specifically with traumatic memory; effective in appropriate structured form. Avoidance strengthens PTSD.
Myth 4: "Benzodiazepines are a good treatment for PTSD"
VA/DoD 2023, APA, NICE — Benzodiazepines contraindicated; worsen PTSD course, impair cognitive processing, high addiction potential.
Myth 5: "Forgetting traumatic memories is the main goal"
The goal is not forgetting but functioning with memory — emotional reaction decreases, but memory remains. The patient can talk about trauma without distress.
